children.name: Carcharhinus
children.description: The largest shark genus, with about 35 species of requiem sharks ranging from the familiar reef, bull, and blacktip sharks to deep-sea and oceanic species found in tropical and subtropical seas worldwide.

children.name: Carcharhinus leucas
children.description: The bull shark, also known as the Zambezi shark in Africa and Lake Nicaragua shark in Nicaragua, is a species of requiem shark commonly found worldwide in warm, shallow waters along coasts and in rivers. It is known for its aggressive nature, and presence mainly in warm, shallow brackish and freshwater systems including estuaries and (usually) lower reaches of rivers. Their aggressive nature has led to ongoing shark-culling efforts near beaches to protect beachgoers, which is one of the causes of bull shark populations continuing to decrease.

children.name: Carcharhinus melanopterus
children.description: The blacktip reef shark is a species of requiem shark, in the family Carcharhinidae, which can be easily identified by the prominent black tips on its fins. Among the most abundant sharks inhabiting the tropical coral reefs of the Indian and Pacific Oceans, this species prefers shallow, inshore waters. Its exposed first dorsal fin is a common sight in the region.

children.name: Carcharhinus amblyrhynchos
children.description: The grey reef shark or gray reef shark is a species of requiem shark, in the family Carcharhinidae. One of the most common reef sharks in the Indo-Pacific, it is found as far east as Easter Island and as far west as South Africa. This species is most often seen in shallow water near the drop-offs of coral reefs.

children.name: Carcharhinus perezi
children.description: The Caribbean reef shark is a species of requiem shark, belonging to the family Carcharhinidae. It is found in the tropical waters of the western Atlantic Ocean from Florida to Brazil, and is the most commonly encountered reef shark in the Caribbean Sea. With a robust, streamlined body typical of the requiem sharks, this species is difficult to tell apart from other large members of its family such as the dusky shark and the silky shark.

children.name: Carcharhinus limbatus
children.description: The blacktip shark is a species of requiem shark in the family Carcharhinidae. It is common to coastal tropical and subtropical waters around the world, including brackish habitats. Genetic analyses have revealed substantial variation within this species, with populations from the western Atlantic Ocean isolated and distinct from those in the rest of its range.

children.name: Carcharhinus longimanus
children.description: The oceanic whitetip shark is a large requiem shark inhabiting the pelagic zone of tropical and warm temperate seas. It has a stocky body and iconic elongated rounded fins, with white tips. The species is typically solitary, though they may gather in large numbers at food concentrations.

children.name: Carcharhinus falciformis
children.description: The silky shark, also known by numerous names such as blackspot shark, gray whaler shark, olive shark, ridgeback shark, sickle shark, sickle-shaped shark and sickle silk shark, is a species of requiem shark, in the family Carcharhinidae, named for the smooth texture of its skin. It is one of the most abundant sharks in the pelagic zone, and can be found around the world in tropical waters. Highly mobile and migratory, this shark is most often found over the edge of the continental shelf down to 50 m (164 ft).

children.name: Carcharhinus obscurus
children.description: The dusky shark is a species of requiem shark, in the family Carcharhinidae, occurring in tropical and warm-temperate continental seas worldwide. A generalist apex predator, the dusky shark can be found from the coast to the outer continental shelf and adjacent pelagic waters, and has been recorded from a depth of 400 m (1,300 ft). Populations migrate seasonally towards the poles in the summer and towards the equator in the winter, traveling hundreds to thousands of kilometers.

children.name: Carcharhinus brachyurus
children.description: The copper shark, bronze whaler, or narrowtooth shark is a species of requiem shark found mostly in temperate latitudes. It is distributed in a number of separate populations in the northeastern and southwestern Atlantic, off southern Africa, in the northwestern and eastern Pacific, and around Australia and New Zealand, with scattered reports from equatorial regions. The species can be found from brackish rivers and estuaries to shallow bays and harbors, to offshore waters 100 m (330 ft) deep or more.

children.name: Carcharhinus galapagensis
children.description: An inquisitive reef shark concentrated around remote oceanic islands, first described from the Galápagos and often the dominant predator at such isolated seamounts.

children.name: Carcharhinus brevipinna
children.description: The spinner shark is a type of requiem shark, in the family Carcharhinidae, named for the spinning leaps it makes as a part of its feeding strategy. This species occurs in tropical and warm temperate waters worldwide, except for in the eastern Pacific Ocean. It is found from coastal to offshore habitats to a depth of 100 m (330 ft), though it prefers shallow water.

children.name: Carcharhinus plumbeus
children.description: A stocky coastal shark with a notably tall first dorsal fin, one of the most heavily fished species for its valuable fins and a mainstay of public aquaria.

children.name: Carcharhinus albimarginatus
children.description: A large reef shark distinguished by the bright white margins on all its fins, an aggressive and dominant predator on Indo-Pacific drop-offs and seamounts.

children.name: Carcharhinus amboinensis
children.description: A heavy-bodied inshore shark closely resembling and often confused with the bull shark, frequenting murky coastal and estuarine waters of the Indo-West Pacific.

children.name: Carcharhinus acronotus
children.description: A small, slender western Atlantic shark distinguished by the dusky blotch at the tip of its snout, commonly found over sandy bottoms and seagrass beds.

children.name: Carcharhinus altimus
children.description: A large, deep-dwelling shark with a notably long, rounded snout and large dorsal fin, ranging along continental slopes in tropical and warm-temperate seas.

children.name: Carcharhinus amblyrhynchoides
children.description: A slender, fast-swimming requiem shark of the Indo-West Pacific, often mistaken for the grey reef shark but distinguished by its more pointed snout and white-tipped dorsal fin.

children.name: Carcharhinus borneensis
children.description: A rare and poorly known small shark historically recorded from Borneo, with a slender body and long, pointed snout; possibly extinct.

children.name: Carcharhinus cautus
children.description: A small, timid coastal shark of northern Australia and Papua New Guinea, named for its skittish behaviour when approached.

children.name: Carcharhinus coatesi
children.description: A small whaler shark restricted to northern Australia and Papua New Guinea, recently elevated from synonymy with C. dussumieri.

children.name: Carcharhinus dussumieri
children.description: A small, slender Indo-Pacific shark of the continental shelf, distinguished by a white stripe on its flank and a long, pointed snout.

children.name: Carcharhinus fitzroyensis
children.description: A small, common shark of northern Australia found in shallow estuaries and mangrove creeks, often seen in turbid inshore waters.

children.name: Carcharhinus hemiodon
children.description: A small, thick-bodied shark of the Indo-West Pacific considered critically endangered and possibly extinct, known from very few historical specimens.

children.name: Carcharhinus humani
children.description: A recently described small requiem shark from the western Indian Ocean, closely related to the blackspot shark but with distinct tooth and fin morphology.

children.name: Carcharhinus isodon
children.description: A slim, fast-swimming shark of the western Atlantic with smooth-edged teeth and a reputation for leaping when hooked, named for its finely serrated dentition.

children.name: Carcharhinus leiodon
children.description: A rare, little-known shark of the western Indian Ocean distinguished by its smooth-edged teeth and black-tipped fins, first described as recently as 2008.

children.name: Carcharhinus macloti
children.description: A small, slender shark of the Indo-West Pacific with a characteristically long, hard, pointed snout and large eyes, common in shallow coastal waters.

children.name: Carcharhinus obsoletus
children.description: A recently described species known only from historical specimens collected in the South China Sea, believed to be possibly extinct.

children.name: Carcharhinus porosus
children.description: A small, slender shark of tropical American waters named for the prominent pores around its mouth, inhabiting shallow coastal and estuarine habitats.

children.name: Carcharhinus sealei
children.description: A small, distinctive Indo-Pacific shark marked by a prominent black spot at the tip of each pectoral fin, found on shallow coral reefs.

children.name: Carcharhinus signatus
children.description: An oceanic species of the Atlantic that migrates vertically at night to feed near the surface, named for its large green eyes adapted to low-light conditions.

children.name: Carcharhinus sorrah
children.description: A common Indo-Pacific whaler with distinctive black-tipped pectoral fins and a prominent black spot on the upper caudal lobe, frequently caught in inshore fisheries.

children.name: Carcharhinus tilstoni
children.description: A common coastal shark of northern Australia, nearly identical to the blacktip shark but distinguished by vertebral count and genetic markers.

children.name: Carcharhinus tjutjot
children.description: A small requiem shark known only from fish markets in Indonesia, recently recognised as a distinct species and still poorly understood.

children.name: Galeocerdo
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the tiger shark, a highly opportunistic apex predator known for its distinctive dark striped pattern, found in warm tropical and subtropical coastal and oceanic waters worldwide.

children.name: Galeocerdo cuvier
children.description: Galeocerdo cuvier (Tiger shark) is a tiger species found in Africa and Asia and North America and South America and Oceania regions.

children.name: Prionace
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the blue shark, one of the most wide-ranging oceanic sharks, distinguished by its vivid blue dorsum and elongated pectoral fins, circling temperate and tropical oceans.

children.name: Prionace glauca
children.description: Prionace glauca (Blue shark) is a blue species found in Africa and Asia and Europe and North America and South America and Oceania regions.

children.name: Negaprion
children.description: The lemon sharks, two species recognised by their yellowish-brown colouring and preference for shallow coastal habitats including mangroves and coral reefs in the Atlantic and Indo-Pacific.

children.name: Negaprion brevirostris
children.description: Negaprion brevirostris (Lemon shark) is a reef species found in Africa and North America and South America regions.

children.name: Negaprion acutidens
children.description: The Indo-Pacific lemon shark, a slow-moving, site-faithful resident of shallow reefs and lagoons with strongly curved, sickle-shaped fins.

children.name: Triaenodon
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the whitetip reef shark, a slender, sociable shark commonly resting in caves on Indo-Pacific coral reefs, readily identified by its white-tipped dorsal and caudal fins.

children.name: Triaenodon obesus
children.description: Triaenodon obesus (Whitetip reef shark) is a reef species found in Africa and Asia and North America and Oceania regions.

children.name: Rhizoprionodon
children.description: The sharpnose sharks, a genus of small, slender requiem sharks inhabiting inshore tropical and subtropical waters across the Atlantic, Indian, and Pacific Oceans, often common near human-populated coastlines.

children.name: Rhizoprionodon acutus
children.description: Rhizoprionodon acutus (Milk shark) is a reef species found in Africa and Asia and Oceania regions.

children.name: Rhizoprionodon lalandii
children.description: A small, slender shark of South American coastal waters, common in the artisanal fisheries of Brazil and distinguished by a long, pointed snout.

children.name: Rhizoprionodon oligolinx
children.description: A diminutive shark of the Indo-West Pacific with a long, narrowly pointed snout, distinguished from congeners by its fewer labial furrows.

children.name: Rhizoprionodon porosus
children.description: A small inshore shark of tropical American waters with a distinctive long snout and enlarged pores near its mouth, frequently taken as bycatch.

children.name: Rhizoprionodon taylori
children.description: A small, fast-growing shark endemic to northern Australia and Papua New Guinea, one of the smallest members of the genus.

children.name: Rhizoprionodon terraenovae
children.description: A small, prolific shark of the western Atlantic frequently encountered along the coasts of the southern United States and Gulf of Mexico.

children.name: Loxodon
children.description: A monotypic genus for the sliteye shark, a small, slim requiem shark of Indo-West Pacific continental shelves named for the conspicuous notch at the rear of its eye.

children.name: Loxodon macrorhinus
children.description: A slender, large-eyed shark of shallow tropical shelf waters, recognised by the distinctive notch at the hind corner of each eye.

children.name: Scoliodon
children.description: The spadenose sharks, tiny requiem sharks with a flattened, trowel-shaped snout, abundant in inshore waters of the Indian Ocean and western Pacific.

children.name: Scoliodon laticaudus
children.description: One of the smallest requiem sharks, with a broad spade-shaped snout and a highly developed placental link to its young, a reproductive trait rare among sharks.

children.name: Scoliodon macrorhynchos
children.description: A small requiem shark with an elongated, flattened snout, distinguished from the spadenose shark by its longer snout and more widely spaced teeth.

children.name: Glyphis
children.description: The river sharks, a genus of rare and poorly known requiem sharks adapted to turbid tropical rivers and estuaries, among the most threatened of all sharks.

children.name: Glyphis gangeticus
children.description: A critically endangered freshwater shark of the Ganges and other South Asian river systems, with tiny eyes suited to hunting by feel in muddy water.

children.name: Glyphis fowlerae
children.description: A recently described river shark endemic to Borneo, known only from a handful of specimens and critically endangered by habitat degradation.

children.name: Glyphis garricki
children.description: A critically endangered river shark restricted to tidal rivers of northern Australia and Papua New Guinea, with a broad, rounded snout and small eyes.

children.name: Glyphis glyphis
children.description: A rare river shark of northern Australia and New Guinea, recognised by its distinctively narrow, spear-shaped upper teeth and preference for the freshwater reaches of large tropical rivers.

children.name: Glyphis siamensis
children.description: A critically endangered river shark known only from the Irrawaddy River basin in Myanmar, with a flattened head and small eyes adapted for turbid freshwater.

children.name: Isogomphodon
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the daggernose shark, a critically endangered requiem shark of the western Atlantic distinguished by its extremely long, flattened snout and blade-like teeth.

children.name: Isogomphodon oxyrhynchus
children.description: A bizarre-looking shark with an exceptionally long, flattened snout and large fins, endemic to the shallow turbid waters of northern South America and now critically endangered by overfishing.

children.name: Lamiopsis
children.description: The broadfin sharks, two species of medium-sized requiem sharks of the Indo-Pacific, characterised by their broad pectoral fins and preference for turbid coastal waters.

children.name: Lamiopsis temminckii
children.description: A medium-sized shark of the Indian Ocean and western Pacific, distinctive for its unusually broad pectoral fins, now considered endangered by trawl and gillnet fishing.

children.name: Lamiopsis tephrodes
children.description: A little-known broadfin shark of coastal Borneo, formerly confused with L. temminckii and recognised as a distinct species only in modern revisions.

children.name: Nasolamia
children.description: A monotypic genus for the whitenose shark, a distinctive eastern Pacific requiem shark named for the white blotch at the tip of its long, flattened snout.

children.name: Nasolamia velox
children.description: A slender shark of the eastern Pacific with a notably long, flattened snout bearing a characteristic white blotch at its tip, inhabiting coastal and offshore waters from Mexico to Peru.
